adrs.title: Canonical registry is a single JSON document
Features are the targetable units
Issues are plannable work items
Entity-centric registry with a derived graph export
Normalized prefixed IDs
Claim status and claim tier are orthogonal
Feature implementation state is separate from lifecycle state
Immutable boundary and release snapshots
Fail-closed guards
Generated projections are non-canonical
Explicit schema versioning
Portable SSOT core with repo-specific evidence adapters
Enforce max path and filename lengths under `.ssot`
Profiles are reusable feature bundles with derived pass status
SPECs own typed ADR links
Downstream assurance-language ceilings for feature target claim tiers
Out-of-bounds implementation disposition is explicit
Feature implementation is capped by active required claims

status_notes.note: Tentative local release assurance proposal; not accepted or implementation-bound.

adrs.title: Local Release Assurance Remains SSOT Native

status_notes.note: Tentative local release assurance proposal; not accepted or implementation-bound.

adrs.title: Content Addressed Governed Source Snapshots

status_notes.note: Tentative local release assurance proposal; not accepted or implementation-bound.

adrs.title: Local Evidence Bundles Are Derived Release Artifacts
General rules of interpretation govern SSOT meaning
Definitions govern SSOT vocabulary
Claim Tier Gates Govern Core Promotion
Externally Authored Validation Can Satisfy T4

status_notes.note: Accepted as the modeling correction for umbrella and leaf feature inventories.

adrs.title: Feature parent links are inventory composition

status_notes.note: Created to govern safe SSOT CLI delegation through ssot-mcp.
Updated to require explicit CLI/MCP parity, mirrored MCP command-path tools, and drift enforcement.

adrs.title: SSOT MCP and CLI surfaces stay in parity

status_notes.note: Created to govern SSOT MCP worker repair and scoped maturation behavior.

adrs.title: SSOT MCP workers may request registry repair authority

status_notes.note: Created to govern SSOT MCP worker repair and scoped maturation behavior.

adrs.title: Scoped maturation claims ignore unrelated blockers

status_notes.note: Created to govern SSOT MCP worker repair and scoped maturation behavior.

adrs.title: MCP mediated upgrade uses the current runtime

status_notes.note: Created to govern SSOT MCP worker repair and scoped maturation behavior.

adrs.title: Targeted repair can proceed with unrelated baseline drift
Public registry save validates before write

status_notes.note: Created to govern proof-graph-first assurance semantics after downstream MCP and certification closure work.

adrs.title: Proof Graph Is The Canonical Assurance Unit

status_notes.note: Created to govern operator materialization and higher-tier proof-graph closure flows.

adrs.title: Proof Graph Materialization And Certification Closure

status_notes.note: Created to govern scaffold-first feature creation defaults and explicit opt-out behavior.

adrs.title: Feature create defaults to proof-graph scaffolding

status_notes.note: Created to govern forward-only governance-pack schema compatibility.

adrs.title: Pack schema compatibility is forward-only

status_notes.note: Created to govern evidence-centered proof authority for schema 0.8.0.

adrs.title: Evidence Owns Proof Linkage

status_notes.note: Created to enforce that only tigrbl_runtime imports tigrbl_kernel.

adrs.title: Concrete runtime-kernel import boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs a stable semantic center before it can support more transports, more runtimes, and more optimization work.
adrs.title: ADR-0001 - One Semantic Op Model

status_notes.note: Updated package DAG ownership to remove in-core optional Rust parity obligations.

adrs.summary: The repo already has a strong macro-shape, but several architectural gaps come from boundary leakage: declarative layers owning runtime details, runtime owning concrete transport details, or concrete code bypassing compiled/runtime paths.
adrs.title: ADR-0002 - Package DAG and Ownership

status_notes.note: External schema catalog authority moved to tigrbl_spec; spec-first semantics remain.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs a single authoritative declarative layer so that execution, docs, hooks, and compilation are all driven from the same canonical objects.
adrs.title: ADR-0003 - Spec-First Authoritative Spec Layer

status_notes.note: Updated spec merge precedence to require generic monotone roll-up operators.
Updated spec merge precedence summary and monotone operator contract.

adrs.summary: a. framework hard defaults;
adrs.title: Specification merge precedence

status_notes.note: Updated to derive runtime semantics from scope channel and canonical event names.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl sits behind an ASGI-shaped server boundary. The architecture pack is explicit that richer semantics must be projected above `scope`, `receive`, and `send`, not by inventing a new server/app contract.
adrs.title: ADR-0005 - ASGI3 Boundary and Projection Rules

status_notes.note: Updated to keep bindings as selectors of contract classifications.
Updated binding model with per-binding app framing selection rules.
Updated protocol binding model with canonical BindingSpecs, profile/framing separation, and path-first ownership.

adrs.summary: Transport support in Tigrbl must scale horizontally. The architecture pack already normalizes REST, RPC, streaming, websocket, and WebTransport as bindings over the same semantic model.
adrs.title: ADR-0006 - Protocol Binding Model

status_notes.note: Updated support matrix to distinguish intended app framing from implemented support.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs an explicit statement of which protocols are first-class and what first-class means.
adrs.title: ADR-0007 - First-Class Protocol Support Matrix

adrs.summary: The kernel and runtime architecture already depend on a stable atom vocabulary. The registry pack and architecture notes enumerate structural atoms, handler atoms, and transport-related additions.
adrs.title: ADR-0008 - Atom Catalog and Atom Legality

adrs.summary: Tigrbl execution is phase-driven. The active line already has concrete phase names and a real runtime completion fence.
adrs.title: ADR-0009 - Phase Model and Phase Legality

status_notes.note: Updated from example, performance, and options test inventory to include hook lifecycle diagnostics and hookz traceability.

adrs.summary: Hooks are part of the framework contract, not a best-effort extension point. They need deterministic attachment, ordering, and scope rules.
adrs.title: ADR-0010 - Hook Model and Selector Semantics

adrs.summary: The architecture pack treats security dependencies as a distinct class that must execute before ordinary dependencies.
adrs.title: ADR-0011 - Security Deps and Deps Ordering

adrs.summary: Canonical operations depend on database and engine access, but unrestricted engine access inside arbitrary code paths leads to ownership leaks, hidden transaction semantics, and plan/runtime mismatch.
adrs.title: ADR-0012 - DB Guards and Transaction Guardrails

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs a frozen baseline of canonical OLTP operations so that specs, bindings, handler atoms, engines, docs, and parity work all have the same semantic target.
adrs.title: ADR-0013 - Default Canonical OLTP Ops

status_notes.note: Accepted semantic parity update for JSON-RPC batch behavior and parity evidence.
Normalized ADR title while preserving accepted semantic parity body.

adrs.summary: REST and JSON-RPC are distinct transport/binding surfaces, but Tigrbl should not let them drift into different semantic frameworks. The architecture pack repeatedly treats them as different projections over the same semantic operations.
adrs.title: REST and JSON-RPC Semantic Parity

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs a stable contract between canonical operations and engine implementations.
adrs.title: ADR-0015 - Engine Interoperability Contract

adrs.summary: The architecture pack already treats Tigrbl as a compiled-plan system rather than a purely interpreted request dispatcher.
adrs.title: ADR-0016 - Kernel Plan Compilation, Packing, Fusion, and Compaction

adrs.summary: The public authoring surface will continue to use decorators, but decorators are easy places to smuggle in hidden runtime behavior.
adrs.title: ADR-0017 - Decorators Are Declarative Sugar

status_notes.note: Updated from legacy package edge test inventory to govern module/package alias compatibility paths.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl needs shortcuts and aliases for usability, but shortcuts become dangerous when they express capabilities unavailable through the canonical surface or when they grow into independent feature trees. This ADR keeps convenience aligned with declarative canon.
adrs.title: ADR-0018 - Shortcuts and Aliases Expand to Canonical Forms

adrs.summary: The repo needs a reusable design pattern for moving from user-authored inputs to canonical framework objects and then to projections such as docs, bindings, plans, and runtime behavior.
adrs.title: ADR-0019 - Make / Define / Derive Pattern
ASGI Is Projection Input Not Semantic Authority

adrs.summary: The architecture and registry bundle already point toward a certification program rather than an informal feature sprint.
adrs.title: ADR-0021 - Certification Boundary and Promotion Gates

adrs.summary: Tigrbl is intended to remain ASGI-portable, but portability must be made concrete.
adrs.title: ADR-0022 - Tigrcorn / Uvicorn / Hypercorn Interop and Conformance

status_notes.note: Updated with contract-facing exchange aliasing.
Updated by adr:1182: exchange remains separate from stream initiator and carrier legality metadata; client_stream support must be explicitly governed per carrier.

adrs.summary: The repository exposes overlapping exchange tokens across Python bindings, runtime adapters, optional runtime binding specs, and architecture notes.
adrs.title: ADR-0023 - Exchange Model

status_notes.note: Updated to trace local families to contract families.

adrs.summary: After the semantic, binding, phase, and exchange layers are defined, Tigrbl still needs a stable runtime classification for units of work.
adrs.title: ADR-0024 - Runtime Family Model

adrs.summary: Once the runtime taxonomy is normalized, Tigrbl needs a runtime-owned transport abstraction so handlers and execution loops can remain transport-neutral where possible.
adrs.title: ADR-0025 - OpChannel and Runtime IO Abstraction

adrs.summary: The repository adopts `.ssot/registry.json` as the authoritative machine-readable truth model for planning, features, claims, tests, evidence, boundaries, releases, ADRs, and specs.
adrs.title: ADR-0001 Certification Truth Model

adrs.summary: Status: Proposed
adrs.title: Tigrbl ADR Set

status_notes.note: PathSpec-owned address composition supersedes route-backed spec normalization; legacy route registration remains compatibility only.

adrs.summary: directly governed tests show that route registration, RPC exposure, and docs visibility are tightly coupled and must be governed by one binding-first source.
adrs.title: ADR-0027 - Binding-First REST Decorators and Route-Backed Spec Normalization

adrs.summary: The repository currently exposes convenience APIs through both `factories` and `shortcuts`. That boundary only stays coherent if one namespace owns construction helpers and the other acts purely as a convenience re-export layer.
adrs.title: ADR-0028 - Factories Are Canonical Construction Surfaces

adrs.summary: Documentation authority is partitioned by surface:
adrs.title: ADR-0029 - Documentation Support Uses Canonical Metadata Sources

adrs.summary: The current failing slice in `pkgs/core/tigrbl_tests/tests` shows that AppSpec-driven assembly, imperative harness assembly, kernel bootstrap, and Uvicorn-backed REST / JSON-RPC execution are drifting apart. The failures are not isolated smoke regressions; they indicate that multiple construction paths are no longer preserving the same compiled semantic outcome.
adrs.title: AppSpec, Harness, and Uvicorn Projections Preserve Canonical Outcomes

adrs.summary: Security parity tests cover both runtime and docs surfaces; parity requirements must be explicit at the decision level.
adrs.title: Status, Auth, and Error Projections Derive from Canonical Outcomes

adrs.title: OAS 3.1 security-scheme-first architecture

status_notes.note: Updated ASGI-first strategy with Uvicorn protocol-mode evidence and active runner boundary language.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl remains ASGI-first and server-agnostic for framework-owned behavior.
adrs.title: ASGI-first / server-agnostic runtime strategy

adrs.summary: CLI contract tests validate target loading, target error behavior, and server support expectations as one cohesive contract.
adrs.title: CLI contract and server-selection contract

adrs.summary: Docs UI behavior must be stable without conflating UI concerns with payload or runtime conformance contracts.
adrs.title: Docs UI contract

status_notes.note: Public operator support is now explicit and excludes facade authoring helpers other than get_schema().

adrs.summary: Operator-surface tests validate both runtime behavior and docs projection consistency; both need explicit boundary language.

adrs.title: Next-target datatype/table program activation

adrs.summary: The active `0.3.19.dev1` line already carries the datatype/table next-target program. The transport-dispatch repair work is a separate architectural effort that changes dispatch ownership, transport binding materialization, and JSON-RPC ingress identity. It needs its own governed track, its own boundary snapshot, and its own sequencing before code delivery starts.
adrs.title: ADR-1045 - Transport-Dispatch Track Boundary and Sequencing

status_notes.note: Concrete (scope, path, method) routing is now canonical for the public contract; JSON-RPC endpoint identity remains an internal or derived detail for multiplexed mounts and compilation.

adrs.summary: JSON-RPC is multiplexed: many operations share one concrete ingress such as `/rpc`. The binding model already treats transport support as op-centric metadata, but JSON-RPC currently lacks a first-class endpoint identity that can distinguish multiple mounted multiplexed surfaces without hard-coding concrete paths into every operation binding.
adrs.title: ADR-1046 - Endpoint-Keyed Multiplexed Transport Bindings

adrs.summary: The active line drifted into transport-specific bypasses: app-local HTTP dispatch, direct JSON-RPC endpoint logic, and executor-level lookup/matching. That breaks the intended ownership split between concrete ingress, runtime/executor, and the kernel plan.
adrs.title: ADR-1047 - KernelPlan-Owned Transport Dispatch

adrs.summary: The runtime, harness, and AppSpec parity lanes depend on a stable kernel identity,
adrs.title: Kernel priming, singleton, and OpView cache ownership

adrs.summary: The repository already freezes canonical OLTP verbs, but the operator-surface expansion introduced
adrs.title: Canonical non-OLTP verb families

status_notes.note: Updated from example, performance, and options test inventory to cover methodz/hookz/kernelz diagnostics stability and operation diagnostics projection.

adrs.summary: The framework exposes diagnostics endpoints such as `/system/healthz`, `/system/methodz`, `/system/hookz`, and `/system/kernelz`, and tests treat those endpoints as part of the public surface. The repo does not currently define every stable diagnostics payload, when warnings may be emitted, or how diagnostics inherit the configured system prefix.
adrs.title: Diagnostics surface contract and stability boundary

status_notes.note: Concrete (scope, path, method) routing is now canonical for the public contract; JSON-RPC endpoint identity remains an internal or derived detail for multiplexed mounts and compilation.

adrs.summary: The canonical public routing identity is the transport-agnostic tuple `(scope, path, method) -> op`.
adrs.title: Canonical Path-Keyed Transport Routing Tuple

status_notes.note: Documents the active-line decision that tigrbl_core._spec owns the canonical spec layer while public facade exposure remains a separate projection boundary.
Updated from legacy package edge test inventory to cover AppSpec/RouterSpec nested composition and TableSpec model_ref.

adrs.summary: `tigrbl_core._spec` is the canonical typed contract layer for Tigrbl's spec-owned surfaces.
adrs.title: tigrbl_core._spec Layer Nature and Authority

status_notes.note: Documents the active-line decision that tigrbl_base owns the canonical base layer while top-level facade exposure remains a separate projection boundary.

adrs.summary: `tigrbl_base` is the canonical base-class layer for Tigrbl's inheritance-oriented contract surfaces.
adrs.title: tigrbl_base Layer Nature and Authority

adrs.summary: Tigrbl already exposes middleware as part of the operator surface and the `_spec` layer, but the current SSOT set does not yet govern middleware as its own extension surface.
adrs.title: Middleware Extension Surface Contract

status_notes.note: Narrows ADR-1055 to architectural ownership and namespace separation; companion specs own bootstrap timing and namespace details.

adrs.summary: Tigrbl exposes concrete DDL behavior through `tigrbl_concrete.ddl`, `tigrbl.ddl`, and bootstrap entrypoints such as `initialize()` and `bootstrap_dbschema()`.
adrs.title: DDL surface ownership and initialization boundary

status_notes.note: Updated from legacy package edge test inventory to cover Bootstrappable compatibility projection through tigrbl.orm.mixins.

adrs.summary: `tigrbl_orm.orm.mixins` already provides reusable ORM mixins, and `Bootstrappable` already provides default-row seeding behavior. The repo also relies on MRO-based spec collection for mixin-defined columns and policies, but the current SSOT set does not yet define table mixins and bootstrappable defaults as governed surfaces.
adrs.title: Table mixins and bootstrappable defaults

adrs.summary: Concrete app and router owners expose `mount_static()`, and the concrete static runtime already records static mounts and serves files via safe path resolution.
adrs.title: Static files surface contract

adrs.summary: `tigrbl_concrete` exports concrete response classes for JSON, HTML, text, file, redirect, stream, and SSE behavior, and it also exports a background-task helper and a `TransportResponse` facade symbol.
adrs.title: Concrete response type surface boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl already exposes `Depends` as a concrete runtime helper and uses it across REST, JSON-RPC, and system-route builder normalization.
adrs.title: Concrete dependency helper surface contract

status_notes.note: Accepted authority-boundary rule for docs and CI projection surfaces.

adrs.title: Docs and CI are non-authoritative projections
Tigrbl Runtime Semantics Sit Above ASGI

adrs.summary: Tigrbl has multiple first-class extension families whose semantics live below the transport/operator layer. The repo needs one umbrella architectural boundary for those extension families so helper/export decisions do not blur extension authority with operator support.
adrs.title: Extension surface boundary

status_notes.note: Public operator support is now explicit and excludes facade authoring helpers other than get_schema().

adrs.summary: The older operator-surface ADR was too coarse and did not distinguish between facade-exported authoring helpers and explicitly supported public operator surfaces.
adrs.title: Public operator surface boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl uses the word "schema" in multiple domains. Without a dedicated namespace-boundary ADR, helper and DDL docs drift into ambiguity between Pydantic/model schema materialization and database namespace preparation.
adrs.title: Database namespace boundary
Compiled Selector Resolution Is Authoritative
Selector Namespaces And Shadowing Control

adrs.summary: Table profile configuration must model canon op selection, default op bindings, per-op bindings, documentation exposure, and runtime exposure as separate axes.
adrs.title: Table Profile Axes Are Separate

adrs.summary: The active line already uses `POST_EMIT` as a real completion fence, but the public hook phase vocabulary stops at `POST_RESPONSE`.
adrs.title: Transport completion fence and POST_EMIT legality

adrs.summary: Tigrbl already distinguishes user-facing runtime/operator contracts from lower-layer spec/base authorities, but repo-local maintenance workflows need a separate boundary so maintainers can evolve them without widening the supported user-facing surface area.
adrs.title: Private maintainer surface boundary

adrs.summary: Tigrbl exposes several top-level helper functions for composing tables, hooks, handlers, schemas, and routers. Those helpers are useful for framework authoring and assembly, but they are not the same category of support as stable operator-facing transport, docs, or request/response contracts.
adrs.title: Private authoring surface boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests exercise operation declarations attached through internal decorators, external binding, app and router owners, plain objects, multiple table targets, and dynamic attachment after owner construction. Existing ADRs already say decorators are declarative sugar and that specs are authoritative, but they do not explicitly freeze the placement boundary for operation declarations.
adrs.title: Op declaration attachment boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover aliasing canonical verbs, preserving canonical core handlers, overriding canonical behavior through explicit aliases, and using custom aliases and targets. Existing shortcut and alias ADRs establish that aliases expand to canonical forms, but the tests reveal a sharper architectural line: alias is exposure identity, target is semantic identity.
adrs.title: Alias versus target resolution boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover persist overrides, token normalization, custom-target persist behavior, and handler-chain ordering. Existing ADRs discuss database guardrails and canonical operation semantics, but persist policy needs an explicit boundary so future transport or decorator changes do not reinterpret persistence behavior locally.
adrs.title: Op persist policy and handler chain boundary

adrs.summary: operation contract tests cover REST default op verbs, REST/RPC parity for default operations, `create` versus `bulk_create`, `clear` versus `bulk_delete`, and non-bulk-capable fallback behavior. Existing ADRs freeze the default canonical OLTP set and REST/RPC parity, but they do not explicitly decide how default-operation collisions and capability gaps are resolved.
adrs.title: Default op collision and capability precedence

adrs.summary: operation contract tests exercise `OpSpec` effects across schemas, columns, defaults, storage, REST routes, RPC methods, core CRUD handlers, hooks, atom injection, and system-step registry behavior. Existing ADRs establish a spec-first architecture and one semantic operation model, but they do not explicitly name the materialization side-effect boundary that these tests rely on.
adrs.title: OpSpec materialization side-effect boundary

status_notes.note: Accepted transport bypass removal update for compiled mapping dispatch and boundary evidence.

adrs.summary: The transport boundary tests assert that old `tigrbl.transport` modules, dispatcher files, gateway runtime bypasses, and top-level dispatch exports remain absent. Existing KernelPlan dispatch ADRs define the positive owner of dispatch, but the tests also need a negative boundary decision that makes reintroduction of old bypass surfaces explicitly non-conforming.
adrs.title: Transport bypass removal boundary

adrs.summary: The transport-dispatch mapping harness tests freeze a deterministic plan order and assert that default canonical operations become visible from collected model declarations. Existing kernel-plan ADRs govern runtime compilation broadly, but this test concern is earlier in the pipeline: mapping model declarations into normalized operation and binding materialization inputs.
adrs.title: Mapping plan compilation boundary

status_notes.note: Updated from legacy package edge test inventory to make pre-TX dependency edge-row verification explicit.

adrs.summary: The pre-transaction dependency tests assert that `secdeps` and `deps` execute in `PRE_TX_BEGIN`, that failures abort before `START_TX`, and that kernel diagnostics expose dependency placement. Existing dependency ordering and database guardrail ADRs cover the broad intent, but the tests rely on a sharper phase and transaction boundary.
adrs.title: Pre-TX dependency execution boundary

adrs.summary: The project already treats registry and release artifacts as canonical JSON data, but it does not yet declare RFC 8785 JCS as the required canonicalization profile for proof-oriented payloads. Without an explicit boundary, serializer drift can produce byte-level instability across runtimes and versions.
adrs.title: RFC 8785 JCS canonicalization boundary

Those tests reflect a documentation architecture where docs are generated from canonical metadata and materialized framework state, then checked against runtime behavior.
adrs.title: Documentation support surface partitioning

adrs.body:
## Decision

Tigrbl canonical AppSpec authoring should be a nested, address-first spec tree for exposed surfaces, while concrete classes and decorators may remain ergonomic lowerers into that tree.

1. `AppSpec` is the root of the authored canonical spec tree.
2. `RouterSpec` groups address namespaces, middleware, nested apps, and path surfaces.
3. `PathSpec` owns concrete address paths, including resource paths, JSON-RPC dispatcher paths, WebSocket paths, SSE paths, WebTransport paths, docs UIX paths, docs payload paths, static mounts, and nested app mounts.
4. `TableSpec` may group table-scoped ops under a containing path without using concrete runtime model classes as canonical identity.
5. `OpSpec` owns semantic operation shape and inherits path/table context from the containing tree node instead of embedding concrete `model=...` or `table=...` runtime objects as canonical identity.
6. Protocol bindings select or invoke an op at the containing path; JSON-RPC bindings therefore receive their dispatcher path from the containing `PathSpec` and their method selector from the binding.
7. Documentation UIX and documentation payload paths are normal `PathSpec` entries; the inclusion policy for OpenAPI, OpenRPC, and JSON Schema artifacts is governed by a docs projection contract, not by the path alone. AsyncAPI generation, mounting, payload emission, and UIX are intentionally unsupported.
8. Engine binding is not path-owned. `AppSpec` owns the named engine inventory, while app, router, table, and op scopes attach to engines by name under the policy in `adr:1130`.
9. Transport scenario coverage is governed by the matrix contract in `spc:2152`; path ownership, protocol binding, framing, exchange, docs projection, static serving, and nested mounts remain separate layers.
10. Websocket binding semantics are governed by `adr:1131` and `spc:2153`: `subprotocols` describe WebSocket handshake negotiation, while `framing` describes post-handshake message interpretation.
11. The transport matrix is a governed SSOT coverage contract, not a new canonical spec graph entity or a required production module. Implementation belongs in the existing canonical spec layers.

## Context

Current discussion exposed two problems in the spec layer. First, concrete runtime classes such as ORM model classes leak into canonical examples through `model=Widget` and `table=Widget`. Second, docs UIX paths and docs payload paths need a way to decide which REST APIs, JSON-RPC methods, websocket surfaces, and other bindings are included in emitted docs artifacts.

The repository already has decisions for one semantic op model, protocol bindings, REST/JSON-RPC parity, endpoint-keyed transport routing, documentation support, and spec-layer authority. This ADR narrows the next planning slice: specify nested canonical AppSpec composition with path-scoped protocol bindings and docs projection selection, while keeping concrete classes as lowerers rather than canonical identity.

## Consequences

- `PathSpec` and docs projection contracts become the planning focus for the next implementation slice.
- Engine inventory and scope-level engine binding are governed separately from path and transport ownership.
- Transport support gaps such as stream path kinds, WS/WSS JSON-RPC symmetry, and WS/WSS NDJSON framing are tracked in an explicit matrix rather than inferred from existing binding classes.
- The matrix constrains implementation and testing, but `PathSpec`, binding specs, docs projection specs, `AppSpec`, `RouterSpec`, `TableSpec`, and `OpSpec` remain the implementation surfaces.
- Websocket support gaps are tracked through explicit binding fields for transport protocol, handshake subprotocols, and post-handshake framing.
- Docs UIX and docs payload surfaces do not need to become large independent authorities merely to expose `/docs`, `/openapi.json`, `/openrpc.json`, `/schemas.json`, or equivalent supported paths.
- AsyncAPI must remain absent from public docs helpers, canonical docs payload kinds, and system auto-mount behavior unless a future ADR explicitly reopens that surface.
- Documentation inclusion rules must be expressible in a projection spec that can include/exclude protocols, routers, paths, tables, op aliases, tags, and RPC methods.
- Existing concrete conveniences may continue to accept runtime classes, decorators, and shortcuts, but their canonical output must lower into a nested spec tree with stable spec-native table/resource identity.

## Rejected Alternatives

- Keep embedding REST path, JSON-RPC endpoint, and documentation projection policy directly on `OpSpec`.
- Treat docs UIX paths as the source of truth for OpenAPI/OpenRPC inclusion.
- Treat `PathSpec` as an engine binding scope.
- Conflate engine inventory with transport scenario coverage.
- Conflate websocket subprotocol negotiation with websocket message framing.
- Add a standalone production `transport_matrix` module as the authority for supported path kinds, protocol bindings, docs projection, static serving, nested mounts, or websocket framing/subprotocol semantics.
- Require graph refs for this slice when nested containment and inherited context are the desired authoring model.
- Treat concrete runtime classes as canonical table/op identity in the spec graph.

## Governed Feature Scope

- `feat:nested-appspec-pathspec-composition-001`
- `feat:path-scoped-protocol-binding-composition-001`
- `feat:docs-projectionspec-contract-001`
- `feat:docs-payload-projection-selection-001`
- `feat:docs-uix-projection-selection-001`
- `feat:shared-jsonrpc-dispatcher-table-crud-parity-001`
- `feat:nested-appspec-multisurface-corpus-fixtures-001`
- `feat:docs-projection-openapi-openrpc-selection-tests-001`
- `feat:canonical-spec-concrete-class-rejection-001`
- `feat:crud-rest-jsonrpc-shared-dispatcher-parity-corpus-001`
- `feat:appspec-engine-inventory-001`
- `feat:enginespec-name-identity-001`
- `feat:engine-scope-name-binding-001`
- `feat:engine-precedence-validation-001`
- `feat:concrete-engine-lowering-to-inventory-001`
- `feat:pathspec-engine-rejection-001`
- `feat:transport-scenario-matrix-001`
- `feat:transport-stream-pathkind-001`
- `feat:transport-ws-wss-jsonrpc-001`
- `feat:transport-ws-wss-ndjson-001`
- `feat:docs-transport-projection-selection-001`
- `feat:wsbindingspec-subprotocols-001`
- `feat:wsbindingspec-framing-001`
- `feat:wsbindingspec-framing-subprotocol-validation-001`
- `feat:ws-jsonrpc-subprotocol-parity-001`
- `feat:ws-ndjson-fail-closed-001`
- `feat:pathkind-websocket-binding-convergence-001`
- `feat:docs-websocket-transport-projection-001`

## Verification Plan

- Add AppSpec corpus fixtures that represent REST, JSON-RPC dispatcher, websocket, SSE, WebTransport, docs UIX, docs payload, static, nested app, middleware, and multi-table surfaces as a nested tree.
- Add engine inventory fixtures proving app/router/table/op engine bindings resolve by name with nearest-scope precedence and no path-owned engine field.
- Add transport matrix fixtures proving supported and planned/fail-closed transport scenarios are explicit.
- Ensure transport matrix fixtures exercise canonical spec entities directly rather than importing or requiring a standalone production matrix module.
- Add websocket binding fixtures proving `proto`, `subprotocols`, `framing`, and path kind convergence are explicit.
- Add fail-closed projection tests proving OpenAPI and OpenRPC include only the surfaces selected by the docs projection spec, and AsyncAPI remains unsupported.
- Add parity tests proving default CRUD table ops can lower into both REST paths and a shared JSON-RPC dispatcher path without embedding concrete model classes in canonical specs.

features.body: Feature scope: centralize ASGI projection helpers and require runtime adapters to use them. Valid scenarios include scope projection to normalized metadata, receive projection to canonical request/message/stream/session/datagram events, send projection with completion fences, and unsupported ASGI messages failing closed or remaining trace-only. Invalid scenarios include ASGI messages bypassing kernel taxonomy, server extensions becoming semantic authority, or projection inventing delivery guarantees not declared by the binding.

features.body: Feature scope: annotate atom execution events with atom_id, attempt_id, runtime_plan_id, trace_id, and relevant semantic owner ids. Valid scenarios include atom trace entries that can be drilled back to the compiled atom, operation attempt, runtime plan, table, router, and app. Invalid scenarios include atom execution appearing only as an unlabeled callable or handler qualname. Invariants: atom execution records preserve atom_id, attempt_id, and runtime_plan_id, atom correlation does not cross attempt/session scope without explicit parentage, and missing atom identity fails trace/qlog conformance.

features.body: Feature scope: introduce and enforce canonical op_id as the semantic operation identity. Valid scenarios include one Widget.create op_id shared by REST, HTTP JSON-RPC, WebSocket JSON-RPC, and supported stream/session aliases. Invalid scenarios include transport-specific operation identities, generated handler names leaking into traces or replay, or docs publishing ids that differ from runtime plans. Invariants: one op_id represents one semantic operation, transport selectors are aliases, and op_id is stable across docs, plans, traces, replay records, rollups, and certification fixtures.

features.body: Feature scope: create and propagate concrete runtime ids including attempt_id, session_id, stream_id, datagram_id, engine_session_id, transaction_id, trace_id, request_id, connection_id, client_id, replay_id, and original_attempt_id where applicable. Valid scenarios include unique stream ids within a session, unique attempt ids within runtime execution, and replay records that distinguish replay_id from original_attempt_id. Invalid scenarios include concrete id collisions within parent scope, missing engine_session_id parentage, or replay records that cannot identify the original attempt. Invariants: concrete ids are unique within declared parent scope, cross-scope references are explicit, and required concrete ids fail closed when missing from trace, replay, leakage, and rollup surfaces.

features.body: Feature scope: compare documentation projections and compiled runtime plans for canonical operation identity parity. Valid scenarios include OpenAPI/OpenRPC documents publishing the same op_id used by runtime plans, traces, replay, and rollups. Invalid scenarios include generated docs using transport-specific names or handler names that cannot be reconciled with runtime op_id values. Invariants: docs do not mint independent operation identity, runtime plans are the source for semantic identity, and snapshot tests fail on divergent ids.

features.title: Documentation Runtime Operation ID Parity

features.body:
Canonical documentation support includes both mounted documentation endpoints and framework-compatible in-memory publication surfaces that downstream tooling may inspect directly on the live app or router object.

For OpenAPI specifically, this feature covers publication of the current document through `openapi()` and compatibility exposure through `openapi_schema` when downstream consumers introspect the in-memory object instead of fetching `/openapi.json`.

The compatibility contract requires route mutations to invalidate stale cached OpenAPI state so the in-memory publication surface and the mounted JSON surface continue to represent the same document family for a given runtime-plan revision.

Evidence remains anchored in the mounted docs integration tests and the dedicated OpenAPI schema cache compatibility regression tests.
features.description: Tracks supported documentation outputs, metadata ownership, mounted docs surfaces, and framework-compatible in-memory publication for OpenAPI, OpenRPC, and JSON Schema. AsyncAPI is intentionally unsupported.
features.title: Canonical documentation support surfaces

features.description: Documentation payloads and UIX select included REST, RPC, websocket, stream, SSE, WebTransport, static, and mount surfaces through docs projection policy.
features.title: Docs projections select transport surfaces explicitly

features.title: Docs UIX projection selection

features.title: Documentation Publication Exposure Policy

features.body: Feature scope: Wrap engine/session acquisition and transaction atoms with state checks and engine_session_id ownership. Valid scenarios include session acquisition, transaction begin, flush, commit or rollback, and close under one attempt. Invalid scenarios include committing another attempt transaction, reusing a closed engine session, or silently running rollback-required operations on engines with undeclared rollback support. Invariants include no engine session leakage, no engine downgrade without declaration, and deterministic commit/rollback reporting.

features.body: Feature scope: add binding-aware HTTP body projection. Valid scenarios include REST and HTTP JSON-RPC bindings receiving complete unary request bodies, stream bindings receiving ordered stream.chunk.received events, end-of-body becoming a declared completion signal, and abort/backpressure metadata being preserved where available. Invalid scenarios include treating stream bodies as one opaque request without policy, losing chunk boundaries during retry or replay, or omitting completion state.
features.description: Project HTTP bodies as unary request input or stream chunks according to binding kind.
features.title: HTTP Body Stream Projection

features.description: Govern HTTP and HTTPS JSON-RPC as strict JSON-RPC document framing, separate from generic JSON and NDJSON variants.

features.description: Govern HTTP and HTTPS REST app-level framing policy separately from runtime support, with JSON as the current mature framing and other payload framings gated by explicit support evidence.

features.description: Fence idempotency records with protected operation effects so duplicates cannot observe partial commits or stale rollback state.

features.description: Scope idempotency keys by semantic owner, concrete parentage, principal/tenant where available, binding/runtime plan, session, and engine session where applicable.

features.description: Reject idempotency-key reuse with semantically different payload, selector, owner scope, principal, operation, or binding.

features.description: Compile operation-level idempotency policies without inferring them from transport, HTTP method, engine, or handler naming.

features.description: Allow retry and replay to consume idempotency only under declared operation policy with original-attempt parentage.

features.description: Return declared stable duplicate behavior for idempotent attempts, including original normalized result, already-applied response, or stable tombstone where declared.

features.body: Feature scope: Add attempt state metadata to runtime context, diagnostics, and trace surfaces. Valid scenarios include create/read/update/delete/list/custom operations with one attempt_id and one normalized lifecycle. Invalid scenarios include side effects without attempt_id, retries that duplicate effects despite an idempotency key, and attempts that skip declared states. Invariants include deterministic parentage, idempotency correlation where declared, and no lifecycle transition outside the state machine.
features.description: Track each operation attempt through legal runtime lifecycle transitions with stable parentage and trace identity.
features.title: Operation attempt state machine

features.description: Tracks operation security emission from secdeps across generated OpenAPI/OpenRPC documentation and runtime security behavior. Preserves legacy feature body from feat:oas-005. Evidence roots: `pkgs/core/tigrbl_tests/tests/unit/test_openapi_documentation_security_behavior.py`, `pkgs/core/tigrbl_tests/tests/unit/test_openrpc_documentation_security_behavior.py`, `pkgs/core/tigrbl_tests/tests/unit/test_docs_security_parity.py`

features.description: Govern proof-preserving compaction of completed runtime records while retaining identity, drillback, replayability, diagnostics, leakage prevention, and deterministic rollup behavior.
features.title: Runtime Compaction

features.description: Declare deterministic rollup operators and invalid mixed-scope behavior for compacted runtime fields.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Algebra

features.description: Emit deterministic leakage-safe diagnostics and fail closed for unsupported, lossy, mixed-scope, unfenced, or non-drillback compaction.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Diagnostics Fail Closed

features.title: Runtime Compaction Drillback Manifest

features.description: Prevent compaction of open attempts, open transactions, in-flight sessions, and unfenced effects.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Effect Fences

features.description: Preserve semantic and concrete runtime identity across compacted outputs and source ranges.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Identity Preservation

features.description: Require explicit compaction policy, support grade, source ranges, retention, and sink declaration before compaction runs.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Policy Declaration

features.description: Reject mixed schema, runtime plan, transport, session, engine session, app, tenant, or nondeterminism scopes unless governed merge policy permits them.
features.title: Runtime Compaction Scope Compatibility

features.body: Feature scope: Normalize completion fence metadata across protocol adapters and prevent unsupported delivery claims. Valid scenarios include emitted result recording a local completion fence and terminal attempt state. Invalid scenarios include claiming peer delivery without evidence, silently falling back between framings, or completing a closed session through a payload event. Invariants include bounded delivery guarantees, unsupported framing fail-closed behavior, retry/replay attempt correlation, and no undocumented lifecycle transition.
features.description: Make response, stream, datagram, and message completion semantics explicit and traceable.
features.title: Runtime completion fence contract

features.description: Define the core same input plus same state plus same plan equals same normalized semantic outcome invariant.
features.title: Runtime Determinism Invariant

features.body: Feature scope: Add kernel-level lifecycle definitions, runtime validators, and protocol adapter projections for a shared execution contract. Valid scenarios include REST/JSON-RPC/WebSocket/stream/WebTransport/datagram attempts moving only through legal lifecycle states. Invalid scenarios include undocumented transitions, unsupported framing fallback, cross-session leakage, and cross-engine-session leakage. Invariants include deterministic normalized lifecycle reporting, fail-closed invalid transitions, and traceable IDs across app, router, table, op, atom, attempt, session, stream, datagram, engine session, and trace.
features.description: Publish and enforce the common runtime execution lifecycle across atoms, kernel plans, runtime dispatch, protocol adapters, and engine/session boundaries.
features.title: Runtime execution contract

features.description: Python runtime executors expose the governed favicon and documentation UI endpoint contract: favicon routes behave as browser favicon routes, Swagger UIX is hosted at /docs, and Lens UIX is hosted at /lens. Rust/runtime-binding endpoint parity is retired under adr:1185.
features.title: Runtime executor documentation endpoint parity

features.description: Govern first-class runtime frame envelopes, codec registry lookup, core codec behavior, strict JSON-RPC framing, SSE/WebSocket codecs, WebTransport inner codec dispatch, and binding-policy integration.
features.title: Runtime Frame Codec

features.description: Replace runtime string-switch framing dispatch with an explicit codec registry and fail-closed unknown codec behavior.
features.title: Runtime Frame Codec Registry

features.description: Add first-class runtime Frame and FrameContext models with stable frame kind, payload, content, lane, and concrete instance metadata.
features.title: Runtime Frame Envelope

features.description: Implement distinct JSON, NDJSON, text, bytes, binary, and stream codec variants without collapsing byte, text, binary, or record-boundary behavior.
features.title: Runtime JSON NDJSON Text Bytes Codecs

features.description: Validate JSON-RPC 2.0 request, response, error, and batch documents while rejecting JSON, NDJSON, and partial JSON-RPC substitutes.
features.title: Runtime JSON-RPC Codec Strictness

features.description: Runtime-owned hook legality enforcement target for protocol-runtime implementation.
features.title: Runtime-owned hook legality enforcement

features.body: Runtime organization is an internal package-structure feature: tigrbl_runtime keeps public compatibility imports stable while large channel and executor modules are split into focused runtime helpers. It does not move authority to atoms or kernel in this slice; those package-boundary ownership changes remain separately governed.
features.description: Keep runtime ASGI channel and executor context imports source-compatible while splitting implementation into focused runtime helper modules.
features.title: Runtime package organization compatibility shims

features.body: Feature scope: maintain a validated identity parentage graph across semantic and concrete ids. Valid scenarios include attempt_id linking to op_id, table_id, router_id, and app_id; stream_id linking to session_id; transaction_id linking to engine_session_id and attempt_id; and replay_id linking to original_attempt_id. Invalid scenarios include session leakage, engine-session leakage, missing parentage, or cross-scope references without explicit validation. Invariants: every concrete runtime event has stable identity and parentage, mismatched parentage fails closed, and trace, replay, qlog, and rollup surfaces preserve drillback.
features.description: Maintain validated parentage across semantic ids and concrete runtime ids for attempts, sessions, streams, datagrams, engine sessions, transactions, traces, replay, and rollups.
features.title: Runtime Parentage Graph

features.description: Govern deterministic runtime aggregate summaries that preserve identity dimensions, operator algebra, scope compatibility, drillback, diagnostics, leakage prevention, and comparability.
features.title: Runtime Rollup

features.description: Emit deterministic leakage-safe diagnostics and fail closed for unsupported operators, missing policies, mixed scopes, lossy summaries, redaction failures, and missing drillback.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Diagnostics Fail Closed

features.description: Write rollup manifests that preserve source digests, grouping dimensions, policy ids, operator ids, redaction policy, schema/plan identity, and replay/audit/compaction drillback.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Drillback Manifest

features.description: Summarize effects, effect fences, errors, diagnostics, idempotency, retry, replay, transport equivalence, and trace/qlog correlation without leaking raw sensitive state.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Effect Diagnostic Summaries

features.description: Preserve semantic and concrete runtime identity dimensions across aggregate summaries.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Identity Dimensions

features.description: Declare deterministic field-level rollup operators and invalid mixed-scope behavior.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Operator Algebra

features.description: Require explicit rollup policy, grouping dimensions, source ranges, output grade, redaction, and target sink before summary generation.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Policy Declaration

features.description: Reject mixed schema, plan, transport, session, engine session, app, tenant, or nondeterminism scopes unless a governed merge policy permits them.
features.title: Runtime Rollup Scope Compatibility

features.title: Runtime SSE And WebSocket Frame Codecs

features.body: Feature scope: assign and preserve stable semantic ids for compiled objects including app_id, router_id, table_id, op_id, atom_id, hook_id, binding_id, runtime_plan_id, and schema_id. Valid scenarios include the same compiled app yielding stable semantic ids across REST, JSON-RPC, WebSocket, stream, and WebTransport projections where supported. Invalid scenarios include transport-specific selector names changing semantic ids or trace records reporting only handler names. Invariants: semantic ids are stable across transports for the same compiled app, semantic ids are distinct from concrete execution ids, and runtime plans, docs, traces, replay records, and rollups reference the same semantic ids.
features.description: Assign stable semantic ids to compiled app, router, table, operation, atom, hook, binding, runtime plan, and schema objects.
features.title: Semantic IDs For App Router Table Operation And Atom

features.body: Feature scope: allowlist canonical metadata fields and route unsupported server metadata to trace-only storage where preservation is required. Valid scenarios include normalized client/server identity, selected subprotocol, and governed TLS or ALPN metadata. Invalid scenarios include server-specific fields becoming canonical lifecycle, delivery, or identity state without a projection rule, metadata crossing session or engine-session scope, or unsupported metadata being silently accepted.
features.description: Govern admission of server-specific ASGI metadata into canonical runtime metadata or trace-only metadata.
features.title: Server Metadata Normalization

features.body: Feature scope: compile selector alias indices that map transport-specific selectors to canonical op_id values. Valid scenarios include multiple selectors resolving to the same semantic operation with selector namespace provenance. Invalid scenarios include hidden shadowing, ambiguous selector resolution, or selector conflicts that silently choose a winner. Invariants: selectors are aliases rather than identities, conflicts are namespace-aware, and ambiguous or unsupported selector aliases fail closed.

features.body: Feature scope: normalize websocket connect, receive, send, close, and disconnect through ASGI projection rules. Valid scenarios include websocket.receive projecting to message.received and websocket disconnect preserving session lifecycle state. Invalid scenarios include WebSocket receive bypassing runtime event taxonomy, unsupported framing fallback, or close/disconnect causing undocumented lifecycle transitions.

features.body: Feature scope: normalize WebTransport session, stream, and datagram events from ASGI-like server extension messages. Valid scenarios include stream receive projecting to stream.chunk.received with session_id and stream_id, datagram receive projecting with datagram_id or declared datagram identity, and session events preserving lifecycle state. Invalid scenarios include WebTransport stream payloads being treated as generic messages, cross-session stream leakage, missing stream/datagram identity, or unsupported server extension messages being accepted as canonical semantics.

features.body:
This feature tracks symmetric WS and WSS JSON-RPC binding declarations, including explicit `framing=jsonrpc` semantics and JSON-RPC-compatible websocket subprotocol handling.

It is intentionally scoped to binding parity and validation on the active line. Frozen current-target certification remains a separate support-level question governed by the support matrix and release boundary documents.

features.body:
This feature tracks the canonical declaration of websocket post-handshake message framing independently from handshake subprotocol negotiation.

The canonical framing vocabulary is broader than the current mature runtime framing-atom closure. This feature therefore covers declaration and normalization semantics rather than implying that every declared websocket framing token is already a first-class runtime surface.

Application reaction to contract semantic events

features.description: Tigrbl consumes contract channel_lifecycle states and maps them to operation, transaction, hook, retry, audit, webhook, and API-error behavior without protocol-specific branching. Protocol terms from HTTP/2 RFC 9113, HTTP/3 RFC 9114, QUIC RFC 9000, RFC 8441, and RFC 9220 are rejected at the application reaction boundary.
features.title: Application channel lifecycle reaction

features.description: Encode WebTransport lane identity as initiator, direction, and ordinal bits with uint8 or uint16 width selected from the session max_streams policy, and enforce the per-session stream cap at runtime.
features.title: WebTransport lane ID provisioning

features.description: Binding resume policy compilation and application-level resume ledger support for stream-capable Tigrbl transports.
features.title: Binding stream reconnect resume runtime

features.description: Govern and enforce stream initiator, direction, carrier kind, extension context, exchange, and app framing legality before runtime codec dispatch.
features.title: Protocol stream initiator legality

features.description: Model plain HTTP/3 request, push, control, QPACK, and extension stream classes without treating h3 as a new semantic binding or WebTransport substitute.
features.title: H3 non-WebTransport stream taxonomy

features.description: Preserve client-initiated versus server-initiated WebTransport bidirectional stream metadata alongside existing lane-local inner framing and lane-id direction metadata.
features.title: WebTransport bidi initiator metadata

features.description: First-class inbound webhook event ingress surface that lowers through PathSpec, OpSpec, and HttpRestBindingSpec.
features.title: Inbound webhook surface

features.description: Lower inbound webhook authoring to PathSpec resource paths, custom OpSpec handlers, and POST HttpRestBindingSpec selectors.
features.title: Inbound webhook PathSpec lowering

features.description: Govern inbound webhook signature verification, timestamp tolerance, replay protection, idempotency keys, and acknowledgement policy.
features.title: Inbound webhook signature and idempotency policy

features.description: Ergonomic authoring helper for inbound webhook endpoints that normalizes to canonical PathSpec, OpSpec, and POST HttpRestBindingSpec composition.
features.title: Inbound webhook authoring helper

features.description: Runtime-managed outbound event delivery to remote endpoints, reclassified from outbound webhook delivery to callback delivery.
features.title: Outbound callback delivery

features.description: Establishes Python as the only supported in-core Tigrbl runtime authority and retires Rust parity as a core release obligation.

lifecycle.note: Rust retirement governance is active for the Python-only Tigrbl runtime boundary.

features.title: Python-only runtime authority

features.description: Retires tigrbl_kernel Rust spec, plan, and compile surfaces as supported in-core compiler paths.

lifecycle.note: Rust retirement governance is active for the Python-only Tigrbl runtime boundary.

python.reason: Rust kernel retirement verifies removed Rust surfaces; Python runtime behavior is governed by feat:python-only-runtime-authority-001.

features.title: Rust kernel surface retirement

features.description: Retires Rust executor parity tests, runtime demos, docs references, and benchmark lanes as core Tigrbl obligations.

lifecycle.note: Rust retirement governance is active for the Python-only Tigrbl runtime boundary.

python.reason: Rust runtime parity retirement verifies removed Rust obligations; Python runtime behavior is governed by feat:python-only-runtime-authority-001.

features.title: Rust runtime parity retirement

features.description: Refresh the spec surface matrix and provenance inventory to include inbound webhook taxonomy, outbound callback delivery, TableProfileSpec, and concrete table profile classes.
features.title: Surface matrix webhook and table profile refresh

features.description: Emit explicit DeprecationWarning messages from deprecated tigrbl and tigrbl_concrete transport compatibility modules.
features.title: Transport compatibility deprecation warnings

features.description: Expose JSON-RPC envelope schemas from a non-transport namespace and keep deprecated transport model imports as compatibility re-exports.
features.title: JSON-RPC schema namespace

features.description: Prove REST and JSON-RPC runtime dispatch does not import deprecated transport compatibility modules and remains on concrete app/router, runtime, kernel, and atom frame codec surfaces.
features.title: Transport hot path boundary

features.description: Track documentation, examples, import audit, and replacement namespace readiness before deprecated transport modules may be removed.
features.title: Transport compatibility removal readiness

issues.description: Closed for the explicit 0.3.19 certification boundary: unfinished active-line rows remain tracked outside the frozen candidate boundary, while in-boundary rows are implemented and covered.
issues.title: Active line certification closure is blocked

issues.description: The concrete defineAppSpec and defineRouterSpec surfaces do not expose core canonical fields such as engines, engine_name, paths, docs payload/UIX nodes, response defaults, and related canonical graph structure. This forces authors back toward legacy flat composition and prevents the canonical graph from being the normal authoring path.
issues.title: Concrete AppSpec and RouterSpec factories cannot express the canonical graph

issues.description: DocsProjectionSpec, DocsPayloadSpec, and DocsUixSpec exist as canonical spec entities, but OpenAPI and OpenRPC builders still infer inclusion from runtime routes and models instead of applying projection policy from the canonical graph. Docs payload and docs UIX path nodes remain spec-only rather than authoritative.
issues.title: Docs projection specs are not wired to OpenAPI and OpenRPC emission

issues.description: AppSpec.engines, engine_name validation, and nearest-scope precedence exist in tigrbl_core, but concrete traversal and runtime resolution still read legacy raw engine attributes and ignore canonical engine inventory during materialization. Multi-engine AppSpec scenarios are therefore not runtime-true.
issues.title: AppSpec engine inventory and engine_name precedence are not runtime-authoritative

issues.description: OpSpec still carries legacy transport-shaping fields such as expose_routes, expose_rpc, http_methods, and path_suffix while the canonical design is path-owned and binding-owned. This overlap keeps lowering logic model-centric and weakens canonical graph clarity even when validation succeeds.
issues.title: Legacy op-owned transport fields overlap the path-owned canonical graph

issues.description: tigrbl_core defines RouterSpec.paths, PathSpec kinds, and docs/static/mount nodes, but the concrete app and runtime do not lower that path-owned canonical graph into executable REST, JSON-RPC, stream, SSE, websocket, webtransport, docs, static, or mount surfaces. The canonical graph currently validates without serving as the execution graph.
issues.title: Canonical PathSpec graph is not lowered into concrete runtime surfaces

issues.description: Planned transport and docs projection tests exist for REST, HTTP JSON-RPC, stream, SSE, websocket, WSS, WS JSON-RPC, WSS JSON-RPC, WS NDJSON fail-closed, WSS NDJSON fail-closed, and WebTransport, but the concrete lowering path is not closed enough for those scenarios to certify spec-to-runtime parity. The AppSpec transport matrix therefore remains incomplete as an implemented boundary.
issues.title: Canonical AppSpec transport matrix is not closed through runtime parity

issues.description: Package buildability/importability and package-coordinate traceability now have implemented features, published claims, passing tests, and passed evidence. The broader whole-repo certification gap remains tracked by the whole-repo gap report and by the xfail/runtime closure blocker.
issues.title: Certifiable package proof chain gap

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, POST /rpc calling Tenant.list without an Authorization header returns HTTP 200 with a JSON-RPC result payload. If generated JSON-RPC methods are protected by default, unauthenticated calls should fail with an authentication or authorization error. If generated JSON-RPC methods are public by default, that public policy must be explicit in OpenRPC.
issues.title: Generated JSON-RPC read allows unauthenticated access

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, GET /tenant without an Authorization header returns HTTP 200 OK with tenant records. If generated CRUD routes are protected by default, the request should fail with 401 or 403. If generated CRUD routes are public by default, that public policy must be explicit in the generated contract.
issues.title: Generated REST read allows unauthenticated access

issues.description: JSON-RPC table-backed CRUD dispatch allows Thing.create-style requests with missing required create params to reach persistence, causing database NOT NULL failures and leaking SQLAlchemy/DBAPI details, SQL text, table/column names, and bound parameters in the JSON-RPC -32602 response. Framework must validate generated operation input before persistence and sanitize persistence exceptions at the JSON-RPC boundary.
issues.title: JSON-RPC create with missing required fields leaks persistence internals

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, /openapi.json exposes generated CRUD operations with components.securitySchemes absent and zero operations carrying security requirements. Runtime and contract policy must agree: protected operations should emit security schemes and requirements; public operations should be explicitly limited and documented.
issues.title: OpenAPI generated operations do not declare authentication

issues.description: Against a service built on tigrbl==0.4.0 and observed tigrbl_runtime==0.1.12.dev1, /openrpc.json exposes generated JSON-RPC methods with an empty components.securitySchemes object and zero methods carrying security requirements. Runtime and OpenRPC contract policy must agree: protected methods should declare the security scheme and requirement; public methods should be explicitly identified as unauthenticated.
issues.title: OpenRPC generated methods do not declare authentication

issues.description: Direct package-coordinate feature rows now exist for every active package coordinate under pkgs; the remaining work is broader package-level claim/test/evidence depth, not missing coordinate traceability.
issues.title: Package coordinate feature traceability gap

issues.description: Downstream tests pass but emit repeated PydanticDeprecatedSince20 warnings because Tigrbl schema generation can pass legacy example metadata directly to pydantic.Field instead of json_schema_extra. This is a Pydantic v2 compatibility issue and a likely Pydantic v3 break if left unresolved.
issues.title: Pydantic v2 Field example deprecation warning

issues.description: Published tigrbl==0.4.1.dev3 declares unpinned split-package dependencies, so a normal pip install can resolve the facade package at 0.4.1.dev3 while split packages such as tigrbl-core, tigrbl-base, tigrbl-concrete, tigrbl-runtime, and related packages resolve to 0.4.0. In that environment import tigrbl fails with ImportError: cannot import name DocsPayloadKind from tigrbl_core._spec. The release lane must pin or otherwise coordinate split package versions so the public facade imports consistently from installed artifacts.
issues.title: tigrbl 0.4.1.dev3 resolves inconsistent split package imports

issues.description: The split xfail closure boundaries are frozen, but certification is blocked until selected xfail tests pass without xfail markers and linked features are implemented.
issues.title: Tigrbl xfail closure implementation blocked

issues.description: Callback and webhook terminology is currently ambiguous across HookSpec aliasing, post-emit side effects, outbound HTTP callback delivery, and the intended first-class inbound webhook surface. The repo needs explicit vocabulary that callbacks are HookSpec terminology unless additional semantics are introduced, outbound webhook delivery is outbound callback delivery, and inbound webhook is the first-class webhook object.
issues.title: Callback and webhook vocabulary is ambiguous

issues.description: The current tigrbl_runtime.webhooks surface compiles outbound endpoint delivery plans, builds and signs payloads, retries delivery, and records dead-letter outcomes. Under the agreed taxonomy this is outbound HTTP callback delivery, not the canonical first-class webhook surface. The implementation and proof rows must be reclassified or renamed without losing compatibility.
issues.title: Outbound webhook runtime is misclassified as first-class webhook

issues.description: The intended first-class webhook surface is inbound: an external system POSTs to a Tigrbl endpoint. The repo currently lacks a WebhookSpec that lowers to OpSpec plus HttpRestBindingSpec(method=POST) with payload schema, signature policy, idempotency policy, ack policy, and handler binding.
issues.title: First-class inbound WebhookSpec is absent

issues.description: Once inbound WebhookSpec exists, authoring helpers such as webhook_ctx or webhook(...) should lower cleanly to the WebhookSpec, OpSpec, and REST binding model. Until then, webhook authoring remains implicit and users must hand-compose lower-level REST operations and validation hooks.
issues.title: Inbound webhook authoring helper is absent

issues.description: POST_RESPONSE is a governed hook phase that currently runs before EGRESS_SHAPE and EGRESS_FINALIZE, while POST_EMIT is a runtime-owned completion fence and not a public hook phase. Documentation and surface inventory should state this explicitly so callbacks are not mistaken for app lifespan handlers, well-known resources, or post-transport public hooks.
issues.title: POST_RESPONSE and POST_EMIT semantics are not documented clearly

issues.description: docs/spec-surface-matrix.md and docs/spec-surface-matrix.xlsx still show only generic TableSpec/Table rows and do not document TableProfileSpec, concrete table profile classes, inbound webhook intent, or the outbound callback delivery correction. The matrix should add table-profile inventory rows/sheet and avoid adding CallbackSpec when callbacks are HookSpec terminology.
issues.title: Spec surface matrix omits table profiles and webhook taxonomy corrections

issues.description: The current first-class webhook tests cover outbound delivery, not inbound POST acceptance. New contract tests should cover WebhookSpec lowering to POST routes, payload validation, signature rejection, idempotency handling, successful acknowledgement responses, and generated docs/schema visibility.
issues.title: Inbound webhook contract tests are missing

issues.description: test_first_class_webhook_delivery_contract.py proves outbound HTTP delivery behavior but its naming implies the canonical first-class webhook object. The test and registry rows should be renamed or recast as outbound callback delivery coverage, leaving first-class webhook tests for inbound POST semantics.
issues.title: Outbound webhook tests use first-class webhook naming

issues.description: Branch coverage and security-negative tests still import removed tigrbl.runtime compatibility symbols or catch a legacy runtime exception stub. Tests must import kernel planning from tigrbl_kernel, executor APIs from tigrbl_runtime.executors, and HTTPException from tigrbl_typing.status.exceptions so package boundaries remain authoritative.
issues.title: Tests import removed runtime compatibility shims

Binding profile tokens do not imply operation activation, docs visibility, public network mounting, or ASGI exposure unless those axes are declared.

specs.body:
## Intent

Define the next-slice contract for nested canonical AppSpec composition, path-scoped protocol bindings, and documentation projection selection.

This contract is not only a validation shape. The nested canonical tree is the
authority that concrete lowering, docs emission, runtime compilation, and
conformance checks must consume.

## Contract

1. `AppSpec` MUST be able to describe a nested canonical tree whose public address surfaces are under `RouterSpec.paths` or equivalent router-owned path composition.
2. `PathSpec` MUST represent concrete address paths, including REST resource paths, JSON-RPC dispatcher paths, WebSocket paths, WSS+JSON-RPC paths, SSE paths, WebTransport paths, docs UIX paths, docs payload paths, static file mounts, and nested app mounts.
3. `TableSpec` entries nested under a `PathSpec` MUST provide table/resource context for contained `OpSpec` entries without requiring `OpSpec.table` to hold a concrete runtime class.
4. Canonical `TableSpec` identity MUST be spec-native, using fields such as `name`, `resource`, and optional `model_ref`; concrete runtime model classes are lowerer inputs, not canonical identity.
5. `OpSpec` entries nested under a table or path inherit containing context and define semantic operation fields such as alias, target, arity, schemas, lifecycle defaults, and response policy.
6. `HttpRestBindingSpec` nested under an op selects the op by HTTP method at the containing `PathSpec` path.
7. `HttpJsonRpcBindingSpec` nested under an op selects the op by JSON-RPC method at the containing dispatcher `PathSpec` path; multiple table groups may share one dispatcher path.
8. WebSocket, WSS+JSON-RPC, SSE, and WebTransport bindings follow the same rule: the containing `PathSpec` owns the address path, and the binding owns the protocol-specific selector or stream semantics.
8a. `WsBindingSpec.subprotocols` owns WebSocket handshake negotiation, while `WsBindingSpec.framing` owns post-handshake message interpretation under `spc:2153`.
9. `DocsProjectionSpec` MUST define documentation inclusion policy separately from docs UIX and payload path exposure. It MUST be able to include and exclude by protocol family, router/path scope, table/resource scope, op alias/target, tags, visibility, and RPC method selector.
10. `DocsPayloadSpec` is a thin payload emission surface that names a supported documentation artifact kind such as OpenAPI, OpenRPC, or JSON Schema and points at a docs projection policy. AsyncAPI is intentionally unsupported and MUST NOT be accepted as a docs payload kind.
11. `DocsUixSpec` is a thin UIX surface that names a UI renderer such as Swagger, Lens, Redoc, Scalar, or equivalent and points at a docs payload path or docs projection policy.
12. Docs UIX paths and docs payload paths MUST be ordinary `PathSpec` entries. They are uniquely spec'd only when their renderer, payload kind, or projection policy differs.
13. OpenAPI emission MUST be selected from the canonical tree through a docs projection that includes appropriate HTTP/REST bindings and excludes non-selected protocol surfaces. Implementations MUST NOT treat incidental mounted runtime routes as the authority for OpenAPI inclusion.
14. OpenRPC emission MUST be selected from the canonical tree through a docs projection that includes appropriate JSON-RPC bindings and RPC method selectors, including shared dispatcher paths. Implementations MUST NOT infer OpenRPC inclusion only by walking runtime route attachment.
15. Concrete class conveniences, decorators, and imperative assembly helpers MAY remain non-flat and ergonomic, but their canonical output MUST normalize to this nested tree before docs generation, runtime compilation, or conformance checks consume it.
16. Concrete lowering MUST materialize runtime REST, JSON-RPC, stream, SSE, websocket, WebTransport, supported docs payload, docs UIX, static, and mount surfaces from this canonical tree. Reconstructing those surfaces from legacy model/router state after canonical lowering is invalid.
16a. Concrete lowering MUST fail closed for AsyncAPI docs payloads and MUST NOT mount `/asyncapi.json` or expose AsyncAPI UIX helpers.
17. Engine binding MUST follow `spc:2151`: `AppSpec` owns named engine inventory, app/router/table/op scopes attach by engine name, and `PathSpec` does not own engine binding.
18. Transport scenario coverage MUST follow `spc:2152`: path kind, protocol binding, exchange, framing, docs projection, static, and mount coverage are tracked as an explicit matrix.
19. Websocket path kinds MUST converge with websocket binding declarations: generic websocket path kinds allow baseline websocket framing, JSON-RPC path kinds require JSON-RPC framing, and NDJSON path kinds remain planned/fail-closed until supported.

## Examples

A table with default CRUD parity may lower into REST collection/member paths and a shared JSON-RPC dispatcher path:

```python
RouterSpec(
    paths=(
        PathSpec(
            path=/widgets,
            tables=(TableSpec(name=Widget, resource=widget, ops=(...REST collection ops...)),),
        ),
        PathSpec(
            path=/widgets/{item_id},
            tables=(TableSpec(name=Widget, resource=widget, ops=(...REST member ops...)),),
        ),
        PathSpec(
            path=/rpc,
            tables=(
                TableSpec(name=Widget, resource=widget, ops=(...JSON-RPC Widget ops...)),
                TableSpec(name=Account, resource=account, ops=(...JSON-RPC Account ops...)),
            ),
        ),
    )
)
```

Docs paths are ordinary path entries whose inclusion policy is delegated to docs projections:

```python
PathSpec(
    path=/openapi.json,
    docs_payload=DocsPayloadSpec(kind=openapi, projection=public-http),
)
PathSpec(
    path=/openrpc.json,
    docs_payload=DocsPayloadSpec(kind=openrpc, projection=public-rpc),
)
PathSpec(
    path=/docs,
    docs_uix=DocsUixSpec(kind=swagger, payload_path=/openapi.json),
)
```

## Traceability

- ADRs: `adr:1129`, `adr:1130`, `adr:1052`, `adr:1080`, `adr:1014`, `adr:1051`
- Companion specs: `spc:2151`, `spc:2152`, `spc:2153`
- Defines features: `feat:nested-appspec-pathspec-composition-001`, `feat:path-scoped-protocol-binding-composition-001`, `feat:docs-projectionspec-contract-001`, `feat:docs-payload-projection-selection-001`, `feat:docs-uix-projection-selection-001`, `feat:shared-jsonrpc-dispatcher-table-crud-parity-001`, `feat:nested-appspec-multisurface-corpus-fixtures-001`, `feat:docs-projection-openapi-openrpc-selection-tests-001`, `feat:canonical-spec-concrete-class-rejection-001`, `feat:crud-rest-jsonrpc-shared-dispatcher-parity-corpus-001`, `feat:pathspec-engine-rejection-001`, `feat:docs-transport-projection-selection-001`, `feat:pathkind-websocket-binding-convergence-001`, `feat:docs-websocket-transport-projection-001`

## Verification Plan

- Add positive corpus fixtures for multi-middleware, nested app, multi-table, REST, JSON-RPC, WSS+JSON-RPC, multiple SSE, WebTransport, docs UIX, docs payload, and static path surfaces.
- Add engine inventory fixtures through `spc:2151` to prove engine binding does not belong to `PathSpec`.
- Add transport matrix fixtures through `spc:2152` to prove every supported and planned/fail-closed transport scenario is explicit.
- Add websocket path kind convergence fixtures through `spc:2153` to prove websocket bindings and containing paths agree.
- Add negative corpus fixtures that reject concrete runtime model objects as canonical table/op identity in normalized specs.
- Add OpenAPI/OpenRPC projection tests proving docs inclusion is determined by `DocsProjectionSpec`, not by incidental mounted paths.
- Add shared JSON-RPC dispatcher tests proving multiple tables can share one dispatcher path while preserving default CRUD parity with REST paths.
